result:
:::writing
There’s a small, stubborn plant growing out of a crack in the sidewalk near my apartment. It shouldn’t be there. The concrete is thick, the summers are brutal, and no one waters anything on that stretch of street unless you count the occasional spilled iced coffee. But every morning it’s still there—two green leaves, a thin stem, angled slightly toward the sun like it knows something the rest of us don’t.

People step over it without noticing. A few have tried to yank it out, maybe out of boredom, maybe out of that quiet human urge to make things look “right.” But the roots must run deeper than expected, because it always comes back, or maybe it never really leaves. It just bends, waits, and continues.

I’ve started timing my walks so I pass by it when the light hits just right. For a few minutes each day, the plant glows—not in some magical way, just ordinary sunlight catching on something alive—but it feels like a small rebellion. Proof that not everything fragile is weak. Proof that persistence doesn’t need to be loud to be real.

It makes me wonder how many other quiet victories are happening all the time. How many things are surviving in places they weren’t meant to, holding on without applause, without acknowledgment. A crack in the sidewalk isn’t much of a home, but it’s enough.

And maybe that’s the whole thing—maybe “enough” is more powerful than we give it credit for.
:::

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
